elements.sent-2411-001: In 1960, Gr\"{u nbaum proved that for any convex body $C\subset\R^d$ and every halfspace $H$ containing the centroid of $C$, one has that the volume of $H\cap C$ is at least a $\frac{1 {e}$-fraction of the volume of $C$.}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently, in 2017, Basu and Oertel conjectured that a similar result holds for mixed-integer convex sets.
elements.sent-2411-003: Concretely, they proposed that for any convex body $C\subset \R^{n+d}$, there should exist a point $\mathbf{x \in S=C\cap(\Z^{n}\times\R^d)$ such that for every halfspace $H$ containing $\mathbf{x}$, one has that \begin{equation}\vol_d(H\cap S) \geq \frac{1}{2^n}\frac{1}{e}\vol_d(S),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{H ^d$ denotes the $d$-dimensional Hausdorff measure.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: While the conjecture remains open, Basu and Oertel proved that the above inequality holds true for sufficiently large sets, in terms of a measure known as the lattice width of a set.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, by following a geometric approach, we improve this result by substantially reducing the threshold at which a set can be considered large.
elements.sent-2411-006: We reduce this threshold from an exponential to a polynomial dependency on the dimension, therefore significantly enlarging the family of mixed-integer convex sets over which the Basu-Oertel conjecture holds true.
elements.sent-2411-007: A classic result by Gr\"{u nbaum~\cite{Grunbaum1960Partitions states that for every convex body $C\subset \R^d$, and for every halfspace $H$ of $\R^d$, one has that \begin{equation}\label{eq:GrunbaumInequality} H\text{ contains the centroid of }C\implies \frac{\mathrm{vol}_d(H\cap C)}{\mathrm{vol}_d(C)} \geq \left( \frac{d}{d+1}\right)^d,\end{equation} where $\mathrm{vol _d(\cdot)$ stands for the usual $d$-dimensional volume \DS{(given by the Lebesgue measure over $\R^d$) .}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq:GrunbaumInequality} H contains the centroid of C\implies \frac{vol_d(H\cap C)}{vol_d(C)} \geq \left( \frac{d}{d+1}\right)^d,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-008: Note that the function $d\mapsto \left( \frac{d {d+1}\right)^d$ is decreasing and it converges to $\frac{1 {e}$ as $d$ grows to infinity.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Thus, the above inequality entails that \begin{equation}\label{eq:GrunbaumInequality-2} H\text{ contains the centroid of }C\implies \frac{\mathrm{vol}_d(H\cap C)}{\mathrm{vol}_d(C)} \geq \frac{1}{e}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:GrunbaumInequality-2} H contains the centroid of C\implies \frac{vol_d(H\cap C)}{vol_d(C)} \geq \frac{1}{e}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-010: This result, nowadays known as Gr\"{u nbaum's inequality, (together with the study of general measures over convex sets, see \cite{Grunbaum1960Partitions,Grunbaum1963Measures ) is at the core of many advances in convex geometry, see, e.g., \cite{Klartag2007ACentral,Stephen2017Grunbaum,Bobkov2010Probability,Boroczky2017Cone-volume,Brzezinski2013Volume,Belloni2008OnTheSymmetry,Lovasz2007TheGeometry,Stephen2019Applications .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: It has also been used in the field of optimization in the study of cutting plane algorithms \cite{Nemirovsky1983Complexity and randomized algorithms \cite{Bertsimas2004Solving,He2008Semidefinite,Dabbene2010ARandomized .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: A particularly interesting application is the study of the information complexity of mixed-integer convex optimization \cite{BasuOertel2017Centerpoints,Basu2023Complexity,Basu2024Information : \AC{if we want to optimize a linear objective function over a convex body that we can access only via a separation oracle, Gr\"unbaum's inequality gives a bound on the minimum number of queries to the oracle required to find the solution.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Motivated by its application to mixed-integer convex optimization, Basu and Oertel~\cite{BasuOertel2017Centerpoints studied how to extend Gr\"{u nbaum's inequality to mixed-integer convex bodies, that is, sets of the form $S:=C\cap(\Z^n\times\R^d))$, where $C$ is a convex body.}}

elements.paragraph-2411-001: A worst-case-like example and the Basu-Oertel conjecture:
elements.sent-2411-020: Let $K\subset\R^d$ be a $d$-dimensional compact cone, $C = [0,1]^n\times K$ and $S = C\cap(\Z^n\times\R^d) = \{0,1\}^n\times K$.

elements.sent-2411-052: In what follows, we consider $n,d\in \N$ and we set $C\subset\R^{n+d}$ to be a convex body, that is, a convex compact set with nonempty interior.
elements.sent-2411-053: We denote by $S = C\cap (\Z^n\times \R^d)$ as the mixed-integer set induced by $C$.
elements.sent-2411-054: It will be useful to adopt the following convention: We identify $\R^{n+d}$ with $\R^n\times\R^d$.
elements.sent-2411-055: We use the letters $z,w$ to denote elements of $\R^n$ and letters $x,y$ to denote elements of $\R^d$.
elements.sent-2411-056: We use bold characters $\mathbf{x ,\mathbf{y}$ to denote elements of $\R^n\times\R^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: For every $r\geq 0$, we denote by $\vol_r$ the $r$-dimensional Hausdorff measure (see, e.g., \cite[Definition 2.1]{Evans2015Measure ).}
elements.sent-2411-059: When $r$ is an integer, $\vol_r$ coincides with the $r$-dimensional Lebesgue measure over each affine space of dimension $r$.
elements.sent-2411-060: For any set $K\subset \R^{n+d}$ we denote by $\dim K$ its Hausdorff dimension (see, e.g., \cite[Definition 2.2]{Evans2015Measure ) which is given as \begin{equation}\dim K = \inf\{ r>0\ :\ \vol_r(K) = 0\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-061: When $K$ is a convex set, the $\dim K$ coincides with the dimension of the affine hull of $K$, $\mathrm{aff (K)$.}
elements.sent-2411-062: So, if $K$ is a convex body and $\dim K = q$, then the usual $q$-dimensional volume of $K$ coincides $\mathcal{H _q(K)$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Thus, in the case where $C\subset\R^{n+d}$ is a convex body and $S = C\cap(\Z^n\times\R^d)$, one has that the measure $\mu(cdot)$ defined in \Cref{eq:MixedInteger-Volume coincides with the normalization of $\mathcal{H ^d$, that is, \begin{equation}\forall A\subset S,\quad \mu(A) = \frac{\sum_{z\in\Z^n} vol_d(A\cap (\{z\}\times \R^d))}{\sum_{z\in\Z^n} vol_d(S\cap (\{z\}\times \R^d))} = \frac{\vol_d(A)}{\vol_d(S)}.\end{equation}.}}
